coredata.dc:title: A carbon dot-based fluorometric probe for oxytetracycline detection utilizing a Förster resonance energy transfer mechanism
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Fu, Yanzhao
Huang, Li
Zhao, Shaojing
Xing, Xuejian
Lan, Minhuan
Song, Xiangzhi
coredata.dc:description: A carbon dot (CD)-based fluorometric probe for oxytetracycline (OTC) detection utilizing a Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) mechanism was firstly developed. Upon addition of OTC, the blue fluorescence of the CDs peaked at 405 nm was quickly quenched, with a new fluorescence peaked at 505 nm attributed to OTC was observed within 30 s. A visual fluorescent color change of the CDs solution from blue to green was discerned under a 365 nm UV light irradiation. The CDs displayed a high sensitivity and selectivity toward OTC with a low detection limit of 0.41 μM. Furthermore, the probe was applied to detect OTC in water, milk, and pork samples with a satisfied recovery.
coredata.dcterms:subject: FRET
Fluorometric probe
Carbon dots
Antibiotics

CDs as a fluorometric probe for OTC
The selectivity of the probe to OTC

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM and (b) AFM images of the CDs.

OTC concentration-dependent (a) absorption and (b) fluorescence spectra of CD aqueous solutions. Inset in (b) shows the fluorescent images of the CDs solution before and after addition of 40 μM OTC under 365 nm UV light irradiation. (c) The Linear relationship between the I505/I405 ratio of the CDs and the OTC concentration. (d) Time-dependent I505/I405 ratio of the CDs with the addition of 40 μM OTC in K2HPO4-NaOH (0.1 M, pH 10) buffer solution.

(a) FT-IR spectra of CDs (black line), OTC (red line), and CDs + OTC (blue line). (b) The absorption spectrum of OTC (red line) and the emission spectrum of CDs (blue line). (c) Time-resolved fluorescence decay spectra of CDs aqueous solution recorded at 405 nm before (red line) and after (blue line) addition of OTC.
Selectivity of the probe to OTC in the presence of (a) antibiotics, (b) amino acids, (c) metal ions, (d) anions. The concentration of antibiotics, amino acids, metal ions, anions and OTC are 40 μM.
Effect of pH on the fluorescence spectra of (a) CDs and (b) CDs + OTC. (c) The I505/I405 ratio of CDs under different pH values in the absence (black line) and presence (red line) of OTC.
The interaction mechanism between CDs and OTC.
Comparison of CD-based fluorescent sensors for OTC detection.
Recovery data of spiked water, milk, and pork samples.

head.ce:title: A carbon dot-based fluorometric probe for oxytetracycline detection utilizing a Förster resonance energy transfer mechanism

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Correspondence to: M. Lan, Shenzhen Research Institute of Central South University, Shenzhen 518057, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shenzhen Research Institute of Central South University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A carbon dot (CD)-based fluorometric probe for oxytetracycline (OTC) detection utilizing a Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) mechanism was firstly developed. Upon addition of OTC, the blue fluorescence of the CDs peaked at 405 nm was quickly quenched, with a new fluorescence peaked at 505 nm attributed to OTC was observed within 30 s. A visual fluorescent color change of the CDs solution from blue to green was discerned under a 365 nm UV light irradiation. The CDs displayed a high sensitivity and selectivity toward OTC with a low detection limit of 0.41 μM. Furthermore, the probe was applied to detect OTC in water, milk, and pork samples with a satisfied recovery.

ce:section.ce:para: Oxytetracycline (OTC) is widely used to treat bacterial infectious diseases caused by both Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria. However, the abuse of OTC in farm livestock leads to water pollution and residues in animal-derived food products, posing a severe threat to human health [1]. The World Health Organization stipulated maximum residue limits of OTC in animal-derived foods and milk of 0.1 mg/kg [2]. Currently available techniques for OTC detection, including high-performance liquid chromatography [3], liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry [4], enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay [5], and electrochemical analysis [6], require expensive equipment and complicated procedures, limiting their practical applications. Therefore, developing novel sensing methods with simple operation, high sensitivity, and selectivity for OTC detection is greatly desired.

In recent years, fluorescent probes have attracted great attention because of their simple procedure and on-line detection feature [7–10]. Kinds of fluorescent materials, including organic fluorescence dyes and inorganic quantum dots have been developed to quantify OTC [11–13]. Although some of them exhibit a high sensitivity to OTC, they also suffer from complicated synthetic routes and long response times, impeding their practical applications. As an emerging fluorescent nanomaterial, carbon dots (CDs) have been widely applied in the environmental, energy, and biosensor fields due to their excellent properties, such as low toxicity, excellent biocompatibility, good photostability and water solubility [14–17]. Various CDs have been synthesized to detect antibiotics, as summarized in Table 1 [18–31]. However, these CD-based probes adopt fluorescence quenching as the signal output, which is susceptible to interference by factors such as instruments efficiency, probes concentration, and environmental conditions [32]. Moreover, most of these probes show a long response time to OTC, which is also not favor to their practical applications.

In contrast, fluorometric probes show simultaneous fluorescence intensity and wavelength variations, which can significantly avoid the above mentioned drawbacks and improve their accuracy by self-calibration [33,34]. Recently, Liu et al. constructed a fluorometric probe to detect tetracycline by mixing Eu3+ ions with CDs, with a LOD of 11.7 nM and a reaction time of 1 min [30]. Yan et al. fabricated the ratiometric fluorescence sensor (MIPs@rCDs/bCDs@SiO2), which had been constructed by the red fluorescent CDs (rCDs) used to responsive signal and the blue fluorescent CDs (bCDs) served as reference signal. Molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) have customized recognition sites complementary to template molecules in shape, size and functional groups. The silica layer could effectively inhibit direct interaction between bCDs and TC to present a trustworthy reference signal for the ratiometric fluorescence detection. The MIPs@rCDs/bCDs@SiO2 was applied to sensitively and selectively detect tetracycline at a low LOD of 1.19 nΜ and response time of 6 min [31]. Despite the fact that a fluorometric probe for OTC sensing can be produced by simply introducing external fluorophores to CDs, the reproducibility of these probes is poor. It is highly desired to develop CDs with intrinsic ratiometric fluorescence spectral response to analyze.

Herein, we firstly report a CD-based fluorometric probe for OTC detection through a Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) mechanism. As demonstrated in Scheme 1 , the surface of the blue fluorescent CDs is rich in amino, carboxyl, and hydroxyl groups. Upon addition of OTC, the multiple hydrogen-bonding interactions and π-π stacking interaction between CDs and OTC narrowed their distance less than 10 nm. Meanwhile, there are a large overlap between the absorption spectrum of OTC and the fluorescence spectrum of CDs, which further boosted the energy transfer from CDs to OTC. As a result, the blue fluorescence of the CDs peaked at 405 nm was efficiently quenched, and a new green fluorescence peaked at 505 nm was gradually increased. Besides, a visual fluorescent color of the CDs solution changed from blue to green. Subsequently, the probe was successfully applied to detect OTC in food and water samples.

Kanamycin, gentamycin, K3PO4, MnCl2·4H2O, FeCl3, FeSO4·7H2O, MgCl2, NiCl2·6H2O, CoCl2·6H2O, KCl, Na2CO3, KSCN, Na2SO3, NaF, KI, Na2S, NaOH, NaCl, alanine, glycine, glutamate, threonine, aspartic acid, proline, and histidine were purchased from Titan Co., Ltd. Streptomycin, K2HPO4, carbenicillin sodium, and glutamic acid were purchased from Xiya Chemical Industry Co., Ltd. Amoxicillin, penicillin, oxytetracycline, glutathione, phenylalanine, proline, and tryptophan were purchased from Heowns Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. All reagents and chemicals were AR grade and used without further purification.

Absorption and fluorescence spectra were collected on Shimadzu UV-2600 and RF-6000 spectrophotometer, respectively. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) was obtained using a M15E-PS/10 (Bruker) IR spectrometer. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) image was performed on Nano-Scope (Bruker). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image were acquired by a JEOL JEM-2100F electron microscope. A Mettler pH-meter was used to perform the pH measurements.

30 μL CDs solution (15 μg/mL) was added into 2 mL K2HPO4-NaOH (0.1 M pH 10) buffer solution to obtain the probe solution. The OTC dissolved in K2HPO4-NaOH (0.1 M, pH 10) buffer solution was added into the probe solution and the fluorescence spectra were collected with an excitation wavelength of 320 nm.

The LOD of OTC was calculated according to following equation:
LOD = 3δ / S
where δ is the standard deviation of the fluorescent intensity ratio (I505/I405) of CDs (n = 20), S is the slope of linear relationship about the fluorescent intensity ratio (I505/I405) of CDs versus the concentration of OTC.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of OTC in actual samples
ce:section.ce:para: Five practical samples (river water, tap water, mineral water, milk, and pork) were selected to assess the sensing performance of the probe. The procedures of the pre-treatment of these real samples were presented in our previous work [8].

Subsequently, 2 mM OTC were prepared by using the pretreated supernatant of the above five real samples as solvent. The recoveries of this method were estimated by spiking with these samples with concentration of 10, 20 and 30 μΜ, and calculated by the follow formula:
Recovery =
Conc . measured in spiked sample
Conc . spiked concentration
× 100 %

The TEM and AFM images shown in Fig. 1a and b revealed that the CDs are uniformly dispersed in water with a mean diameter of ~3.8 nm and a vertical height of ~1.7 nm. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and FT-IR results suggested that the surface of CDs is rich in amino, carboxyl, and hydroxyl groups. The detailed characteristic data of the CDs were presented in our previous work [35].

ce:section.ce:section-title: CDs as a fluorometric probe for OTC
ce:section.ce:para: The application of the CDs as a fluorometric probe for OTC was firstly investigated. As shown in Fig. 2a, CDs have a broad absorption spectrum with an absorption peak at ~220 nm. Upon addition of OTC, two absorption peaks at 320 and 440 nm which are originated from OTC were observed. The fluorescence spectrum of CDs showed a strong emission peak at 405 nm (Fig. 3b) with a fluorescent quantum yield of 3.0% under excitation with λex = 320 nm. However, with the increase in OTC concentration, a decrease in fluorescence intensity at 405 nm was observed, coupled with the appearance of a new emission peak at 505 nm, ascribed to OTC (Fig. 2b). Moreover, under 365 nm UV light, the fluorescent color of CDs solution was changed from blue to green which is easily observed by naked eyes. (Fig. 2b, insets). By plotting the fluorescence intensity ratio (I505/I405) versus the concentration of OTC, a linear relationship (R2 = 0.990) was observed for an OTC concentration range of 0 to 40 μM (Fig. 2c); the LOD was thus calculated to be 0.41 μM.

Subsequently, the reaction time of the probe to OTC was measured by monitoring the time-dependent I505/I405 ratio of the CDs solution after addition of 40 μM OTC. The I505/I405 ratio increased significantly within 30 s following the addition of OTC (Fig. 2d). Further prolongation of the reaction time showed no obvious changes in the intensity ratio, demonstrating that the reaction time of CDs with OTC is less than 30 s, which is much faster than the previously reported works (shown in Table 1).

The sensing mechanism of CDs to OTC was firstly assessed by FT-IR. Peaks indicating the presence of -COOH, -OH, and -NH2 groups on the surface of CDs were observed (Fig. 3a, black line). The FT-IR spectrum of OTC (Fig. 3a, red line) showed four peaks at 1667, 1629, 1580, and 1441 cm−1, characteristic of CC from the benzene ring in OTC, and the peak at 1236 cm−1 was attributed to CO stretching vibration. Finally, the FT-IR spectrum of CDs after addition of OTC showed a red shift in the peak of OH to 3443 cm−1, while the intensity of CO, CC, and CN peaks were weakened (Fig. 3a, blue line). These changes in the FT-IR spectra revealed a strong hydrogen bonding interaction and π–π stacking interaction between the CDs and OTC, which narrowed their distance less than 10 nm.

To further understand the FRET mechanism between CDs and OTC, the absorption spectrum of OTC and the emission spectrum of CDs were collected. As shown in Fig. 3b, the absorption spectrum of OTC overlapped well with the emission spectrum of CDs, the extinction coefficients of CDs and OTC was measured to be 0.0109 mL/μg·cm−1 and 0.0144 μM−1·cm−1, respectively, indicating an efficient FRET process from CDs to OTC. Furthermore, the fluorescence lifetimes of the CDs aqueous solution in the absence and presence of OTC were measured. As shown in Fig. 3c, the CDs shows two exponential decay with the longest fluorescence lifetime of 7.0 ns. However, after adding OTC, the fluorescence lifetime was decreased to 5.7 ns, further demonstrating there is FRET from CDs to OTC.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The selectivity of the probe to OTC
ce:section.ce:para: The selectivity of CDs to OTC was evaluated by monitoring the changes in the I505/I405 ratio before and after addition of other antibiotics, amino acids, metal ions, and anions. As shown in Fig. 4 , the I505/I405 ratios were as low as ~0.3 in the presence of these interfering molecules. However, the addition of OTC led to a significant increase in the I505/I405 ratio from 0.3 to 1.7. We noted that after addition of tetracycline (TC) and doxycycline (DOC), the I505/I405 ratio was increased to around 1.0; this was attributed to the fact that DOC, TC, and OTC have similar molecular structures. However, careful comparison of three antibiotics, OTC contains more hydroxyl groups than TC and DOC, resulting in stronger hydrogen bonding interaction between CDs and OTC, and thus the FRET efficiency is higher for the OTC. Furthermore, the fluorescence quantum yields of the OTC, TC, and DOC are 0.56%, 0.51%, 0.15%, respectively. The higher I505/I405 ratio may be due to a higher QY of OTC than that of TC and DOC. Thus, this fluorometric probe is specific toward OTC sensing among other biological competing species and antibiotics.

To further investigate the application of the probe in practice, the effect of pH on the fluorescence spectra of CDs before and after addition of OTC were also investigated. As shown in Fig. 5a, the change of pH hardly affects the fluorescence spectra of CDs. However, after adding OTC, the green fluorescence peaked at 505 nm was gradually increased with pH range from 4 to 12 (Fig. 5b). The variation of the I505/I405 ratio under different pH solution were summarized in Fig. 5c, in the absence of OTC, no obvious change of the I505/I405 ratio was observed in the pH range from 4 to 12. However, after addition of OTC, the I505/I405 ratio was significantly increased as the pH increased from 7 to 12. While under acidic condition, only a small fluorescence intensity ratio I505/I405 was observed. This is due to the pH-dependent fluorescence of the OTC, i.e., a high fluorescence intensity was obtained in a large pH value solution [36].

In order to assess the application of the fluorometric probe in actual samples, five real samples, i.e., river water, tap water, mineral water, milk, and pork, were selected to analyze. The recoveries on real samples with 10, 20, and 30 μM of OTC spiked were estimated. The recoveries of OTC ranged from 93% to 102% with a satisfactory RSD were obtained, as revealed in Table 2 .

In summary, we proposed a CD-based fluorometric probe for the fast detection of OTC based on the FRET mechanism. With the addition of OTC, the blue fluorescence of the CDs at 405 nm was gradually quenched within 30 s, with the appearance of a new emission peak at 505 nm due to the FRET from CDs to OTC. The fluorescent color of the CDs solution changed from blue to green following the addition of OTC. The CDs probe exhibited a high selectivity and sensitivity to OTC with a LOD of 0.41 μM. Finally, the prepared probe was utilized in the sensing of OTC in real samples. This work may offer a new approach for designing CDs-based fluorometric sensing platform for sensitively and selectively detecting antibiotics and other pollutants.
Prof. M. Lan and Prof. X. Song planed and supervised the project. Y. Fu, L. Huang, and X. Xing designed and carried out the detailed experiments. Dr. S. Zhao analysed and revised the data.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no competing financial interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 61805287), Natural Science Foundation of Hunan Province, China (Nos. 2018JJ3632 and 2019JJ50824), Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities of the Central South University (Nos. 2020CX021, 2019zzts126), and Basic Research Foundation of Shenzhen Science and Technology Innovation (No. JCYJ20180307151245919).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A tailored molecular imprinting ratiometric fluorescent sensor based on red/blue carbon dots for ultrasensitive tetracycline detection

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Liu, X.; Wang, T.; Wang, W.; Zhou, Z.; Yan, Y. A tailored molecular imprinting ratiometric fluorescent sensor based on red/blue carbon dots for ultrasensitive tetracycline detection. J. Ind. Eng. Chem. 2019, 72, 100-106.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A distinctive near-infrared fluorescence turn-on probe for rapid, sensitive and chromogenic detection of sulfate in food

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Duan, C.; Zhang, J.; Hu, Y.; Zeng, L.; Su, D.; Bao, G. A distinctive near-infrared fluorescence turn-on probe for rapid, sensitive and chromogenic detection of sulfate in food. Dyes Pigm. 2019, 162, 459-465.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A ratiometric fluorescent probe for rapid detection of hydrogen sulfide in mitochondria

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Chen, Y.; Zhu, C.; Yang, Z.; Chen, J.; He, Y.; Jiao, Y.; He, W.; Qiu, L.; Cen, J.; Guo, Z. A ratiometric fluorescent probe for rapid detection of hydrogen sulfide in mitochondria. Angew. Chem., Int. Ed. 2013, 52, 1688-1691.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Paper-based microfluidic analytical devices for colorimetric detection of toxic ions: a review

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Srirama, G.; Bhat, M. P.; Patil, P.; Uthappa, U. T.; Jung, H. Y.; Altalhi, T.; Kumeria, T.; Aminabhavie, T. M.; Pai, R. K.; Madhuprasad; Kurkuria, M. D. Paper-based microfluidic analytical devices for colorimetric detection of toxic ions: a review. TrAC, Trends Anal. Chem. 2017, 93, 212-227.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon dots and CdTe QDs hybrid-based fluorometric probe for spermine detection

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Fu, Y.; Wu, S.; Zhou, H.; Zhao, S.; Lan, M.; Huang, J.; Song, X. Carbon dots and CdTe QDs hybrid-based fluorometric probe for spermine detection. Ind. Eng. Chem. Res. 2020, 59, 1723-1729.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A target analyte induced fluorescence band shift of piperazine modified carbon quantum dots: a specific visual detection method for oxytetracycline

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Yang, L.; Zhao, H.; Liu, N.; Wang, W. A target analyte induced fluorescence band shift of piperazine modified carbon quantum dots: a specific visual detection method for oxytetracycline. Chem. Commun. 2019, 55, 12364.
